rulings.Case Title: ELEN MEDER ET AL VS. COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO, HUMAN SERVICES AGENCY,
rulings.Calendar Matter: (Amended) Notice Of Hearing "Writ"
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 1, PETITIONERS ELEN MEDER, WALTER MEDER'S (Amended) Notice Of Hearing "Writ". Petitioners Elen and Walter Meder's petition for writ of mandate is denied. The hearing officer correctly determined that the Meders are currently ineligible to receive benefits under the County Adult Assistance Program. The Meders are German nationals with pending asylum applications. Section 91-4 of the CAAP Manual provides that "[a]n alien has eligible status if he is¿granted asylum¿" The manual further provides that ineligible aliens (such as the Meders) are ineligible for CAAP benefits. The Meders acknowledge that their asylum applications are currently pending and thus their asylum status is not a basis for benefits. The Meders fail to show any other basis to receive CAAP benefits. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling adversely affects, in whole or in part, any party who is not represented by an attorney, then any other party (whether represented by an attorney or not) may submit on the tentative ruling without appearing for the hearing only if the adversely affected self-represented party has stated in writing or orally that it, he or she will not appear at the hearing. Counsel for the City is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: WAYNE RUDEN VS. C.R. BARD, INC., A NEW JERSEY CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Continue Jury Trial
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 13, Motion To Continue Jury Trial. Defendants C.R. Bard, Inc. and Bard Peripheral Vascular, Inc.'s motion to continue trial date and associated dates is granted. The trial is continued to February 11, 2019 and all deadlines are triggered off the new trial date. The Bard defendants have shown good cause for the continuance. A trial continuance is further supported by the tentative ruling granting plaintiff Wayne Ruden's motion for leave to file his proposed fourth amended complaint which, assuming the tentative ruling is confirmed, the Bard defendants likely need some or all of the additional time afforded by the trial continuance to determine whether to file a summary motion directed to the new claim alleged against them and, if they choose to file such a motion, to prepare the motion and have it served and heard in conformity with the Code of Civil Procedure. Counsel for the Bard defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the motion and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Ruden is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the motion and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: WAYNE RUDEN VS. C.R. BARD, INC., A NEW JERSEY CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Leave To Amend Complaint
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 13, PLAINTIFF WAYNE RUDEN's Notice Of Motion For Leave To Amend Complaint. Plaintiff Wayne Ruden's motion for leave to file his proposed fourth amended complaint is granted. Mr. Ruden's proposed claim for fraud by nondisclosure against the Bard defendants appears to be adequately pled and would likely withstand a pleading challenge. Granting leave to amend comports with California's liberal policy allowing amendments to pleadings at any stage of a case. Since the tentative ruling on the Bard defendants' motion to continue trial is to grant a trial continuance to February 11, 2019 and assuming that the tentative ruling is confirmed, there is ample time for defendants to address the new allegations and thus granting the motion does not result in any legal prejudice to the defendants. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Ruden is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the motion and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW PILLET VS. STEPHEN H KENDRICK ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Appearance Of Person Most Knowledgeable At A Designated Place, And Production Of Books, Letters, Papers, And Files Pursuant To C.C.P. Section 1987.1 [Wysteria Holdings, Llc]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFF ANDREW PILLET's Motion To Compel Appearance Of Person Most Knowledgeable At A Designated Place, And Production Of Books, Letters, Papers, And Files Pursuant To C.C.P. Section 1987.1 [Wysteria Holdings, Llc] Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Court overrules the jurisdiction defense proffered by the third party. Wisteria Holdings was a California registered limited liability company when the subpoena was served. Assuming the documents in question exist Plaintiff presents sufficient factual information to support their production. If the documents subject to the subpoena are presently located out-of-state, C.C.P. § 1987.3 permits their production in-state once plaintiff has waived the personal appearance of a custodian of records. The Court intends to rule on the discovery objections of Wisteria Holdings. As to documents claimed to be privileged, a privilege log will be required. Confidentiality concerns will be resolved through a confidentiality protective order. Wisteria's request for monetary sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/jpt

rulings.Case Title: CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC VS. WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P., Webcor Construction, Inc Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (Added For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Purpose Only)
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 5, Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P., Webcor Construction, Inc Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (Added For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Purpose Only - Part 3 of 4) (See, e.g., Hogar Dulce Hogar v. Community Development Commission of the City of Escondido (2003) 110 Cal. App. 4th 1288). Second, the purpose of the delayed discovery rule, which is to ameliorate the harshness of the preclusion of a claim before a claimant knows of the facts supporting its claim, has little applicability to a claim by an owner that a contractor is unlicensed. There is nothing difficult about discovering whether a contractor is or is not licensed and no reason why an owner who wishes to make such a claim should not make an inquiry about the license status of the contractor. Third, application of the discovery rule to a section 7031 claim would defeat the purposes of the short limitations period established by CCP 340(a). Fourth and perhaps most importantly, as suggested by the facts of this case, application of the discovery rule to a section 7031 claim would mean that a contractor could be required to disgorge funds it received years, even decades, after it received those funds. There is absolutely no indication that this is what the Legislature intended. Indeed, there are many statutes which provide for a private claim based on statutory violations that explicitly include language that the claim is subject to a delayed discovery accrual. (3 Witkin, California Procedure (5th ed. 2008 and 2018 supp.) "Actions" 497). Neither section 7031 nor any of the other statutes relating to licensing of contractors includes any such language. The inapplicability of the delayed discovery rule does not, however, allow a contractor to make a misrepresentation or concealment that prevents an owner from discovering a licensing violation. In such an event, the applicable exception to the normal accrual of a section 7031 claim is the doctrine of equitable estoppel, as made clear in the January 9 order. However, as already stated, CDC has not adequately alleged facts to avail itself of that doctrine, nor could it do so given its acknowledgment that it could have known of a licensing violation had it only looked at the license number in the subcontracts and verified that number with CSLB's records. Further, even if the discovery rule did apply to a section 7031 claim, CDC's allegations seeking to avail itself of that rule show that it could not do so as a matter of law. Paragraph 22 of the SAC alleges that more than one year before it filed this case CDC knew that an entity referred to as "Webcor Builders, Inc." claimed to have constructed the Hotel. (continued in Part 4 of 4)

rulings.Case Title: CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC VS. WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANT WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P., WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, INC DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT (Part 1 of 4) Defendants Webcor Construction, L.P. and Webcor Construction, Inc.'s demurrer to all three causes of action in the second amended complaint filed by plaintiff CDC San Francisco LLC is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to all three causes of action. Leave to amend is limited to alleging that unlicensed construction work was performed within a year of the filing of this lawsuit, as more fully explained and limited below. CDC failed to adequately allege either of the two circumstances for which it was granted leave to amend in the order filed January 9, 2018. CDC did not adequately allege in the SAC that "there was unlicensed work that was performed within the one-year statute of limitations period." (Order 2:26-27). CDC's sole effort to do so is in paragraph 23 of the SAC which alleges: "After June 23, 2016, and through the beginning of 2017, the Contractor, pursuant to its Construction Contract, performed work related to the Hotel's windows for which a contractor's license was required." There are several obvious problems with this allegation, most notably the "Contractor" is not identified and there is no allegation that the "Contractor" lacked the required license. Because the demurring parties assert that the allegation is false and they support their assertion with matters that seem to support their assertion but not dispositively for the purposes of a demurrer, any effort by CDC to remedy the deficiencies in paragraph 23 must, at a minimum, identify the nature of the work performed, the day or days the work was performed, who performed the work, why performance of the work required a contractor's license, and that the entity who performed the work lacked the required license. CDC is given leave to make these allegations if it can do so in good faith. Nor did CDC adequately allege in the SAC that "there was a misrepresentation or concealment with regard to the licensing violations that could not be verified through public records." (Order 3:10-11). While paragraph 30 of the SAC alleges that "the Contractor concealed its actual license number from the Construction Contract ¿ [and] concealed its written subcontracts which did show a different expired license number from CDC," quite clearly these "concealments" are of the type that could be verified by public records. Apparently CDC chose not to do so. Indeed, no doubt unintentionally and apparently not realized by the demurring parties, these alleged concealments are precisely the kind of matters that as a matter of law put a sophisticated owner of a $140 million project on inquiry notice that its contractor may not be licensed and, even under the most generous application of the delayed discovery rule as applied to a Business and Professions Code 7031 claim, precludes the application of the delayed discovery rule. (continued in Part 2 of 4) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC VS. WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P., Webcor Construction, Inc Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only)
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 5, Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P., Webcor Construction, Inc Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (Added To Calendar For Tentative Ruling Purposes Only - part 2 of 4) Query: why would a contractor conceal its license number and basic project documents which contain that number unless the contractor is trying to hide that it might be unlicensed? And, since CDC acknowledges that the license for the general contractor in the subcontracts had expired, had CDC acted on its inquiry notice in 2005 or whenever the subcontracts were concealed from it, CDC would have determined that it had a claim for disgorgement per section 7031 more than a decade ago. CDC's allegation in paragraph 27 of the SAC fares no better. Paragraph 27 alleges that "license 899242 (Webcor Construction LP) was automatically terminated during construction when the general partner of Webcor Construction LP disassociated from the partnership." As the demurring parties point out, that allegation is a misstatement of the applicable statute, Business and Professions Code 7076(c), which makes the date of the license termination "the date the written notification is received" by the CSLB. Moreover, judicially noticeable records show that license 899242 did not terminate until after this case was filed. Apparently recognizing that it has not adequately alleged either of the circumstances for which it was granted leave by the January 9 order, CDC argues that the statement in that order that "CDC had constructive notice regarding the Webcor entities' license status when it made its last payment because that information was publicly available" (Order 2:5-6) is contrary to California law. Though not fully explained in the order, the import of the quoted language is that, at least as applied to this case, the delayed discovery rule is inapplicable because at all times CDC was able to determine the licensing status of the general contractor by reviewing public records. CDC has not cited any authority holding that the delayed discovery rule applies to a claim based on a violation of section 7031 or a claim governed by CCP 340(a), nor is this court aware of any such authority. To make explicit what was implicit in the January 9 order, this court holds that the delayed discovery rule is not applicable to a claim based on section 7031. First, the discovery rule is not applicable to all, or maybe even most, claims based on a violation of a statute. (continued in Part 3 of 4) =(302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: CDC SAN FRANCISCO LLC VS. WEBCOR CONSTRUCTION, L.P. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P., Webcor Construction, Inc Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (Added For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Purpose Only)
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 5, Defendant Webcor Construction, L.P., Webcor Construction, Inc Demurrer To 2nd Amended Complaint (Added For Entry Of Tentative Ruling Purpose Only - Part 4 of 4) As of that date in October 2015, based on allegations in its prior complaints which are binding on it for purposes of this demurrer, CDC knew that the entity it contracted with had a different legal name: "Webcor Construction, Inc." As CDC has alleged in paragraph 25 of the SAC, learning information that a different entity constructed the Hotel than the entity it had believed to be the entity who actually constructed the Hotel "caused CDC" to inquire about the license number used by the general contractor in the subcontracts, which led to the filing of this lawsuit. This very same inquiry notice which CDC says occurred in 2017 was also triggered in October 2015, outside the one year limitations period, when CDC learned information, as it alleges in paragraph 22 of the SAC, that a different entity constructed the Hotel than the entity listed in the contract it entered into for the construction of the Hotel. Therefore, even under CDC's own theories of the law and facts, CDC's claims are time-barred unless some construction work requiring a license was performed by an entity who did not have a required license within a year of the date this case was filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)

If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to enforce court two previous orders compelling plaintiff to respond to written discovery is granted to the extent plaintiff shall have one further opportunity to respond to the subject discovery. Responses to the Request for Production of Documents and Form Interrogatories must be responded to fully and without objection within fifteen days of the date of service of this Order with Notice of Entry. The motion for other sanctions is denied without prejudice. Plaintiff is advised failure to comply with this third Discovery Order will result in the filing of a motion for terminating sanctions. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/jpt

rulings.Case Title: J GARCIA CARRION, SA, A CORPORATION ORGANIZED AND ET AL VS. AMERICAN ARBITRATION ASSOCIATION INC. A NEW YORK ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award
rulings.Rulings: The matter is on calendar for Tuesday, July 10, 2018, Line 9, PETITIONERS VINCENT FRIEND AND FRIEND WINE MARKETING, INC.'S Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Confirm Arbitration Award. Petitioners Vincent Friend and Friend Wine Marketing, Inc.'s petition to confirm contractual arbitration award is granted. Mr. Friend is entitled to judgment in his favor against respondents J Garcia Carrion, S.A. and CIV USA Inc., jointly and severally, in the amount of $2,743,903, plus 10% interest thereon from and after July 31, 2018 compounded annually. Judicial review of contractual arbitration awards is limited to "those cases in which there exists a statutory ground to vacate or correct the award." (Moncharsh v. Heily & Blase (1992) 3 Cal.4th 1, 28). JCG has failed to show that any of the statutory grounds apply. JCG's argument that the remedy the arbitrators awarded was specific performance of the 1992 Agreement buyout provision is not supported by the language of the award which expressly says that the sum awarded to Mr. Friend is for JCG's breach of the contract. (Partial Final Award pp. 9, 18, 31). Even if the measure of damages was legally erroneous as not being based on the difference between the buyout price and the market value of the stock and even if this resulted in a windfall to Mr. Friend, an error of law appearing on the face of the award and causing substantial injustice is not a ground to vacate an arbitration award. (Id.) Nor has JCG shown that any of the grounds for correcting a contractual arbitration award applies to this case. There was no evident miscalculation of figures by the arbitrators. The arbitrators did not exceed their powers because they had the authority to resolve the parties' disputes per the contract which gave them jurisdiction over "any controversy or claim arising out of or related to" the Agreement or its breach. (General Agreement Art. 10). Per Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. v. Intel Corp. (1994) 9 Cal.4th 362, 386, the award was "rationally related to the arbitrator's plausible findings as to the subject matter of the contract and the effects of the breach." (Partial Final Award pp. 2, 12). Nor is the correction sought by JCG a matter of form that does not affect the substance of the controversy. Whether Mr. Friend or JCG is entitled to the amount awarded to FWM substantially affects the merits of the controversy. Mr. Friend's conduct after the award was issued is not a ground for correction of the award. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Friend is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative verbatim and a proposed judgment that conforms to the tentative ruling and must bring them to the hearing or email them to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HEK)
